skill.name: firebase-crashlytics
skill.description: Comprehensive guide for Firebase Crashlytics, including provisioning and SDK usage. Use this skill when the user needs help setting up Crashlytics, adding crash reporting, or using the Crashlytics SDK in their application.
skill.summary: Official Firebase guide for setting up and using Crashlytics crash reporting SDK on Android and iOS apps.
skill.author: firebase

security_audit.summary: The skill consists of three markdown documentation files providing setup guides for Firebase Crashlytics on Android and iOS. All 82 static findings are false positives: backtick patterns are markdown code formatting, CLI commands are legitimate Firebase tools (firebase-tools via npx), hardcoded URLs point to official Firebase/Google documentation, the 'weak crypto' finding is a false match on the 'DWARF' debugging format, and system reconnaissance findings are about reading standard project config files (.pbxproj, Info.plist). No prompt injection, malicious code, or suspicious patterns were found.

low_findings.title: Shell command examples in documentation
low_findings.description: Markdown files contain npx firebase-tools CLI command examples wrapped in backtick code formatting. The static scanner flagged these as 'Ruby/shell backtick execution' but they are standard markdown code spans for documentation purposes. Commands shown are legitimate Firebase CLI tools (projects:create, apps:create, apps:sdkconfig) used to provision Firebase resources.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: These are clearly markdown-formatted CLI command examples in a Firebase setup guide, not actual executable code or injection vectors.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded URLs to official Firebase documentation
low_findings.description: The documentation references official Firebase and Google Maven repository URLs for dependency lookups and customization guides. All URLs are to firebase.google.com, maven.google.com, and github.com/firebase domains which are legitimate Firebase/Google properties.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: All URLs point to official Firebase/Google documentation and repositories; no suspicious or external domains detected.

content.user_title: Set Up Firebase Crashlytics for Android and iOS Apps
content.value_statement: Setting up Firebase Crashlytics involves multiple steps across project provisioning, SDK installation, and verification that crash reports flow correctly. This skill provides a structured guide to complete the entire setup process with confidence.
actual_capabilities: Guide project and app provisioning through the Firebase CLI
Provide step-by-step Android SDK setup with Gradle configuration
Provide step-by-step iOS SDK setup with Swift Package Manager
Explain how to add Firebase Crashlytics dependencies using the BoM
Document how to force a test crash to verify the installation works
Reference official Firebase documentation for advanced customization
limitations: Does not support web or Flutter platforms; only Android and iOS are covered
Requires the user to have a Firebase project and app already set up
Cannot execute Firebase CLI commands on behalf of the user; instructions must be run manually
Does not cover advanced custom features like custom keys or non-fatal exceptions in detail

use_cases.title: First-time Crashlytics setup on Android
use_cases.description: Add Firebase Crashlytics to a new or existing Android application from project provisioning through test crash verification.
use_cases.target_user: Android developers new to Firebase Crashlytics

use_cases.title: First-time Crashlytics setup on iOS
use_cases.description: Add Firebase Crashlytics to a new or existing iOS application using Swift Package Manager and verify symbol upload configuration.
use_cases.target_user: iOS developers integrating crash reporting for the first time

use_cases.title: Team onboarding to crash monitoring
use_cases.description: Provide team members with a standard, repeatable procedure to enable Crashlytics across multiple mobile projects.
use_cases.target_user: Mobile team leads and engineering managers

prompt_templates.title: Basic Android Crashlytics setup
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me set up Firebase Crashlytics in my Android app. Walk me through adding the Gradle plugin and the SDK dependency using the Firebase BoM.
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner - first time setting up Crashlytics

prompt_templates.title: iOS Crashlytics with Swift Package Manager
prompt_templates.prompt: Set up Firebase Crashlytics in my iOS app using Swift Package Manager. Include the dSYM upload run script and verify the Build Phases configuration.
prompt_templates.scenario: Intermediate - iOS developer with Xcode experience

prompt_templates.title: Test crash verification workflow
prompt_templates.prompt: After installing Crashlytics, guide me through forcing a test crash, confirming the report appears in the Firebase console, and then removing the test code.

prompt_templates.title: Native crash support on Android
prompt_templates.prompt: My Android app uses C/C++ libraries. Enable native crash reporting in Crashlytics by adding the NDK dependency and configuring the native symbol upload flag in my release build type.

output_examples.input: Add Crashlytics to my Android project-level build.gradle.kts
output: Add the Crashlytics Gradle plugin to the plugins block using the latest version from the Google Maven repository
In your app-level build.gradle.kts, apply the plugin and add the Crashlytics dependency using the Firebase BoM
Run a Gradle sync and build your project to confirm everything resolves correctly

output_examples.input: Verify Crashlytics is working on iOS
output: Add a fatalError call in AppDelegate.swift with a 3-second delay using DispatchQueue
Build and run the app on a device or simulator with the Xcode debugger disconnected
Restart the app to allow the crash report to be sent to Firebase
Check the Crashlytics dashboard or use the Firebase MCP get_report tool to confirm the crash was received
Remove the test crash code once verification is complete

best_practices: Use the Firebase Bill of Materials (BoM) to manage SDK versions consistently across all Firebase dependencies
Remove test crash code from production builds once Crashlytics installation has been verified
Configure dSYM upload in iOS release builds to ensure native crashes can be properly symbolized and debugged
anti_patterns: Hardcoding the Crashlytics SDK version instead of using the Firebase BoM for version management
Leaving the test crash code in the app after verification, causing unnecessary crashes in production
Skipping dSYM upload configuration on iOS, which results in unsymbolicated native crash reports

faq.question: What platforms does this skill support?
faq.answer: This skill supports Android and iOS platforms. It does not cover web or Flutter integrations.

faq.question: Do I need the Firebase CLI to use this skill?
faq.answer: The Firebase CLI is recommended for provisioning projects and apps, but is not strictly required if you already have a Firebase project and configuration files.

faq.question: How do I verify Crashlytics is working after setup?
faq.answer: Add a forced crash to your app with a short delay, run it, then restart the app. The crash report should appear in the Firebase console or via the Firebase MCP get_report tool within a few minutes.

faq.question: Can this skill help with advanced Crashlytics features?
faq.answer: This skill covers the core setup process. For advanced features like custom keys, custom logs, user identifiers, and non-fatal exceptions, refer to the official Firebase documentation links provided.

faq.question: Does this skill work with existing Firebase projects?
faq.answer: Yes. If you already have a google-services.json file for Android or a GoogleService-Info.plist for iOS, the skill will guide you to use your existing configuration.

faq.question: What is the Firebase Bill of Materials (BoM)?
faq.answer: The Firebase BoM lets you manage versions for all Firebase Android libraries in one place, so you do not need to track individual version numbers for each Firebase dependency.
